blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ (١)
When Allah’s help comes with victory, (1)
وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا (٢)
and you see the people entering Allah’s religion in throngs, (2)
فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا (٣)
celebrate the praise of your Lord, and plead to Him for forgiveness. Indeed, He is all-clement. (3)

(Commentary)

This Surah contains a promise to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of victory (النصر) and conquest (الفتح), and that he would see the people entering Islam in wave after wave; it then commands him to engage in glorification (التسبيح), praise (التحميد), and seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) at that time. The Surah is Medinan (مدنية), revealed after the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah (صلح الحديبية) and before the Conquest of Mecca (فتح مكة), as we shall clarify.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ وَالْفَتْحُ — When there comes Allah’s help and the opening (1). The appearance of the word "when" (إِذا), with which the verse begins, indicates the future tense. This requires that the content of the verse be an announcement of the realization of a matter that has not yet occurred. Since the matter being announced is "help" (النصر) and "conquest" (الفتح) — things that would bring joy to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — it constitutes a beautiful promise and glad tidings for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and it is among the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Noble Quran.

The intended meaning of "help" and "conquest" is not their general genus, such that they would apply to every instance where Allah supported His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) against his enemies and made his religion prevail over theirs — such as in his battles, military expeditions, or the faith of the Ansar (الأنصار) and the people of Yemen before this. This is because such a general meaning does not align with His subsequent saying: وَرَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللهِ أَفْواجاً — and you see the people entering Allah’s religion in throngs (2).

Nor is the intended meaning the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah, which Allah the Exalted called a "conquest" (فتح) when He said: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً — Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory (48:1); this is because the second verse of this Surah, in its content, does not apply to that event.

The clearest event to which the "help" and "conquest" mentioned in the verse apply is the Conquest of Mecca (فتح مكة), which was the mother of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) conquests during his lifetime, and the manifest victory by which the structure of polytheism (الشرك) in the Arabian Peninsula was demolished.

This is supported by the promise of victory found in the verses revealed regarding al-Hudaybiyyah: إِنَّا فَتَحْنا لَكَ فَتْحاً مُبِيناً لِيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللهُ ما تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِكَ وَما تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِراطاً مُسْتَقِيماً وَيَنْصُرَكَ اللهُ نَصْراً عَزِيزاً — Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, (1) that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come, and that He may perfect His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path, (2) and that Allah may help you with a mighty help (48:1-3). It is very likely that what is in these verses is a promise of a mighty help (نصر عزيز) linked to the Conquest of al-Hudaybiyyah, which was His (the Exalted) helping His Prophet (ص) against the Quraysh until the Conquest of Mecca occurred two years after the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah.

What has been mentioned is more plausible than interpreting the verse as referring to the people of Yemen accepting the true call and entering Islam without fighting. Thus, the most considered view is that "help" and "conquest" refer to His (the Exalted) helping His Prophet (ص) against the Quraysh and the Conquest of Mecca, and that the Surah was revealed after the Truce of al-Hudaybiyyah and the revelation of Surah al-Fath, but before the Conquest of Mecca.

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللهِ أَفْواجاً — and you see the people entering Allah’s religion in throngs (2). Al-Raghib said: "The throng (الفوج) is a group passing quickly, and its plural is afwaj (أفواج)." [End of quote]. Thus, the meaning of people entering the religion of Allah in throngs is their entering it group after group. What is meant by "the religion of Allah" (دين الله) is Islam; the Exalted said: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ — Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam (3:19).

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَاسْتَغْفِرْهُ إِنَّهُ كانَ تَوَّاباً — then celebrate the praise of your Lord and plead to Him for forgiveness; indeed He is all-clement (3). Since this help and conquest constituted an abasement from Him (the Exalted) for polytheism and an exaltation for monotheism (التوحيد) — or in other words, a nullification of falsehood (إبطال الباطل) and a realization of the truth (إحقاق الحق) — it was appropriate from the first perspective to declare His (the Exalted) transcendence and to glorify Him (تسبيحه). From the second perspective — which is that of a blessing (نعمة) — it was appropriate to laud Him (the Exalted) and praise Him (حمده). Therefore, He commanded him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by saying: فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ — then celebrate the praise of your Lord.

There is another perspective for directing the command toward glorification, praise, and seeking forgiveness together: it is that the Lord (the Exalted) has a right over His servant that the servant should remember Him by His attributes of perfection (صفات كماله) and remember himself by his own deficiency and need. Since this conquest marked the completion of most of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) efforts in removing falsehood and uprooting corruption, he was commanded to remember Him at that time by His majesty (جلاله), which is glorification (التسبيح), and His beauty (جماله), which is praise (التحميد), and to remember the deficiency of his own self and his need for his Lord, which is the request for forgiveness (طلب المغفرة). Its meaning regarding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — while he is already forgiven — is a request for the continuation of forgiveness; for the need for forgiveness in subsistence (بقاء) is like the need for it in origination (حدوثا). Understand this. By this, his gratitude to his Lord (the Exalted) is completed. We have previously discussed the meaning of the "forgiveness of sin" in earlier sections (1).

His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ تَوَّاباً — indeed He is all-clement — is a justification for the command to seek forgiveness, and it is not devoid of encouragement and emphasis.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', narrated from Muqatil: "When this Surah was revealed, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited it to his companions, and they rejoiced and received the glad tidings. But when al-Abbas heard it, he wept. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'What makes you weep, O uncle?' He replied: 'I think that your own death has been announced to you, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'It is as you say.' He lived for two years after that, and he was never seen laughing or joyful thereafter."

I say: This meaning has been narrated in several reports with different wordings. It has been said regarding the indication of this meaning that the context alludes to the completion of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) efforts and struggle and the perfection of his affair; and when perfection is reached, departure is expected.

In the same source, from Umm Salama, she said: "Toward the end of his life, the Messenger of Allah (ص) would not stand, sit, come, or go without saying: سبحان الله وبحمده استغفر الله وأتوب إليه — 'Glory be to Allah and by His praise, I seek forgiveness from Allah and I turn to Him in repentance.' We asked him about that, and he said: 'I have been commanded to do so,' then he recited: إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ وَالْفَتْحُ."

I say: There are several narrations with this meaning, with some variation in what he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to say.

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission to al-Husayn ibn Khalid, who said: "Al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: 'I heard my father narrating from his father (peace be upon him): The first Surah to be revealed was بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ, and the last Surah to be revealed was إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ.'"

I say: Perhaps what is meant is that it was the last Surah to be revealed in its entirety, as has been suggested.

In al-Majma', regarding the story of the Conquest of Mecca: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) made peace with the Quraysh in the year of al-Hudaybiyyah, it was among their conditions that whoever wished to enter into the covenant of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) could do so. Thus, the Khuza'ah entered into the contract of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Banu Bakr entered into the contract of the Quraysh. There had been an old enmity between the two tribes.

Later, fighting broke out between the Banu Bakr and the Khuza'ah. The Quraysh supported the Banu Bakr with weapons, and some from the Quraysh fought alongside them at night, hiding their identity. Among those who personally aided the Banu Bakr against the Khuza'ah were Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl and Suhayl ibn Amr.

Amr ibn Salim al-Khuza'i rode until he reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in Medina — and this was what instigated the Conquest of Mecca. He stood before him while he was in the Mosque among the people and said:

O Allah, I am reminding (1) Muhammad Of the ancient alliance of our father and his father (2) Indeed, the Quraysh have broken their promise to you And violated your confirmed treaty And they killed us while we were bowing and prostrating

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Enough, O Amr.' Then he stood up, entered the house of Maymunah, and said: 'Pour water for me.' He began to wash while saying: 'May I not be helped if I do not help the Banu Ka'b' — they were the kinsmen of Amr ibn Salim. Then Budayl ibn Warqa al-Khuza'i set out with a group from Khuza'ah until they reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They informed him of the losses they suffered and the Quraysh's support for the Banu Bakr against them. Then they turned back toward Mecca. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had said to the people: 'It is as if you see Abu Sufyan coming to strengthen the contract and increase the duration, and he will meet Budayl ibn Warqa.' They met Abu Sufyan at Usfan; the Quraysh had sent him to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to strengthen the contract.

When Abu Sufyan met Budayl, he asked: 'Where have you come from, O Budayl?' He replied: 'I traveled along this coast and through the heart of this valley.' He asked: 'Did you go to Muhammad?' He said: 'No.' When Budayl went on to Mecca, Abu Sufyan said: 'If he had come from Medina, he would have fed [his camel] date-stones there.' He went to the place where Budayl's camel had knelt, took some of its dung, crumbled it, and saw date-stones in it. He said: 'I swear by Allah, Budayl has come from Muhammad.'

Then Abu Sufyan set out until he reached the Messenger of Allah (ص). He said: 'O Muhammad, spare the blood of your people, grant protection (أجر) among the Quraysh, and increase the duration for us.' He said: 'Have you acted treacherously, O Abu Sufyan?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'Then we remain as we were.' Abu Sufyan left and met Abu Bakr, saying: 'Grant protection among the Quraysh.' He replied: 'Woe to you! Can anyone grant protection against the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?' Then he met Umar ibn al-Khattab and said the same to him. Then he left and entered upon Umm Habiba. He went to sit on the carpet, but she rushed to the carpet and folded it. He said: 'O my daughter, do you find this carpet too good for me?' She said: 'Yes, this is the carpet of the Messenger of Allah (ص); you are not to sit on it while you are an impure polytheist.'

Then he left and entered upon Fatima (peace be upon her), saying: 'O daughter of the master of the Arabs, will you grant protection among the Quraysh and increase the duration, so that you may be the most noble lady among the people?' She said: 'My protection is the protection of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).' He said: 'Will you command your two sons to grant protection among the people?' She said: 'By Allah, my two sons have not reached the age to grant protection among the people, and no one grants protection against the Messenger of Allah (ص).' He then said: 'O Abu al-Hasan, I see that matters have become difficult for me, so advise me.' Ali (peace be upon him) said: 'You are the elder of the Quraysh; so stand at the door of the Mosque and grant protection among the Quraysh, then return to your land.' He asked: 'Do you think that will avail me anything?' He said: 'No, by Allah, I do not think so, but I find nothing else for you.' Abu Sufyan stood in the Mosque and said: 'O people, I have granted protection among the Quraysh.' Then he mounted his camel and departed.

When he reached the Quraysh, they asked: 'What is behind you?' He informed them of the story. They said: 'By Allah, Ali ibn Abi Talib did nothing more than play with you; what you said avails us nothing.' He said: 'No, by Allah, I found nothing else.'

The narrator said: Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered the preparations for the war of Mecca and told the people to get ready. He said: 'O Allah, take away the eyes and news from the Quraysh until we surprise them in their lands.' Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah wrote to the Quraysh, but news came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from the heavens. He sent Ali (peace be upon him) and al-Zubayr until they took his letter from the woman; this story has already been mentioned in Surah al-Mumtahanah.

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) appointed Abu Dharr al-Ghifari as his deputy and set out intentionally for Mecca on the tenth of Ramadan in the eighth year with ten thousand Muslims and about four hundred horsemen. Not a single person from the Muhajirun (المهاجرين) or the Ansar (الأنصار) stayed behind.

Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib and Abdullah ibn Umayyah ibn al-Mughirah met the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at Niq al-Uqab, between Mecca and Medina. They sought to enter upon him, but he did not give them permission. Umm Salama spoke to him regarding them, saying: 'O Messenger of Allah, your cousin, your aunt's son, and your brother-in-law.' He said: 'I have no need for them. As for my cousin, he defamed my honor. As for my aunt's son and brother-in-law, he is the one who said to me in Mecca what he said.' When the news reached them, and Abu Sufyan had his son with him, he said: 'By Allah, he shall permit me, or I will take the hand of this son of mine and we will wander the earth until we die of thirst and hunger.' When that reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he felt compassion for them, so he permitted them, and they entered upon him and embraced Islam.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) camped at Marr al-Zahran, and news had been hidden from the Quraysh so that no report of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reached them, Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, Hakim ibn Hizam, and Budayl ibn Warqa went out that night to scout for news. Al-Abbas had said that night: 'O, what a terrible morning for the Quraysh! By Allah, if the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) surprises them in their lands and enters Mecca by force, it will be the destruction of the Quraysh until the end of time.' He went out on the mule of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'I will go out to the Arak trees; perhaps I will see a woodcutter, or a milkman, or someone entering Mecca who can inform them of the position of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so they may come to him and seek security.'

Al-Abbas said: 'By Allah, I was circling the Arak trees, seeking what I had gone out for, when I heard the voices of Abu Sufyan, Hakim ibn Hizam, and Budayl ibn Warqa. I heard Abu Sufyan saying: "By Allah, I have never seen fires like tonight's." Budayl said: "These are the fires of Khuza'ah." Abu Sufyan said: "Khuza'ah is too few for that."'

He said: 'I recognized his voice and said: "O Abu Hanzalah" — meaning Abu Sufyan. He said: "Abu al-Fadl?" I said: "Yes." He said: "At your service, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you, what is behind you?" I said: "This is the Messenger of Allah behind you; he has come with that which you cannot withstand, with ten thousand Muslims."'

He said: 'What do you command me?' I said: 'Ride on the back of this mule so I may seek security for you from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); for by Allah, if he captures you, he will strike your neck.' He rode behind me, and I set out galloping with the mule of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Whenever I passed by one of the fires of the Muslims, they said: 'This is the uncle of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the mule of the Messenger of Allah.' Until I passed by the fire of Umar ibn al-Khattab. He — meaning Umar — said: 'O Abu Sufyan! Praise be to Allah Who has empowered [us] over you without a treaty or contract.' Then he rushed toward the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and I galloped the mule until I burst into the door of the tent, preceding Umar by as much as a beast precedes a slow man.

Umar entered and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, this is Abu Sufyan, the enemy of Allah; Allah has empowered [us] over him without a treaty or contract, so let me strike his neck.' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I have granted him protection.' Then I sat by the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), took hold of his head, and said: 'By Allah, no one shall speak privately with him today besides me.' When Umar persisted, I said: 'Gently, O Umar! By Allah, you only do this because he is a man from the clan of Banu Abd Manaf; had he been from Adi ibn Ka'b, you would not have said this.' He said: 'Gently, O Abbas! Your embracing Islam on the day you did was more beloved to me than the Islam of al-Khattab had he embraced it.' He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Go, for we have granted him security until you bring him to me in the morning.'

He said: 'When morning came, I brought him to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When he saw him, he said: "Woe to you, O Abu Sufyan! Has the time not come for you to know that there is no god but Allah?" He said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, how joining of ties you are, how noble, how merciful, and how clement! By Allah, I thought that if there were another god with Him, he would have availed [us] on the day of Badr and the day of Uhud." He said: "Woe to you, O Abu Sufyan! Has the time not come for you to know that I am the Messenger of Allah?" He said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you; as for this, there is still something in the soul regarding it." Al-Abbas said: "I said to him: Woe to you! Bear the testimony of truth before your neck is struck." So he bore testimony.'

Then he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to al-Abbas: 'Depart, O Abbas, and hold him at the narrow part of the valley until the armies of Allah pass by him.' He said: 'I held him at the spur (خطم) of the mountain at the narrow part of the valley. The tribes passed by him, tribe by tribe, and he would say: "Who are these?" and I would say: "Aslam, Juhaynah, and so-and-so." Until the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed in the "Green Battalion" (الكتيبة الخضراء) of the Muhajirun and Ansar, clad in iron, such that nothing could be seen of them except their eyes. He said: "Who are these, O Abu al-Fadl?" I said: "This is the Messenger of Allah among the Muhajirun and Ansar." He said: "O Abu al-Fadl, the kingdom of your nephew has become great." I said: "Woe to you! It is prophethood (النبوة)." He said: "Yes, then."'

Hakim ibn Hizam and Budayl ibn Warqa came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), embraced Islam, and pledged allegiance to him. When they had pledged allegiance, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent them ahead of him to the Quraysh to call them to Islam. He said: 'Whoever enters the house of Abu Sufyan — which is in the upper part of Mecca — is safe. Whoever enters the house of Hakim — which is in the lower part of Mecca — is safe. And whoever closes his door and withholds his hand is safe.'

When Abu Sufyan and Hakim left the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heading for Mecca, he sent al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam after them, placing him in command of the cavalry of the Muhajirun. He ordered him to plant his banner in the upper part of Mecca at al-Hajun, and said to him: 'Do not leave until I come to you.' Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered Mecca and his tent was pitched there. He sent Sa'd ibn Ubadah with the battalion of the Ansar in his vanguard, and he sent Khalid ibn al-Walid with those who had embraced Islam from Quda'ah and Banu Sulaym, ordering him to enter the lower part of Mecca and plant his banner before the houses.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) ordered them all to withhold their hands and not to fight except those who fought them. He ordered them to kill four individuals: Abdullah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh, al-Huwayrith ibn Nufayl, Ibn Khatal, and Miqyas ibn Subabah. He also ordered them to kill two singing girls who used to sing satires against the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He said: 'Kill them even if you find them clinging to the curtains of the Kaaba.' Ali (peace be upon him) killed al-Huwayrith ibn Nufayl and one of the singing girls, while the other escaped. Miqyas ibn Subabah was killed in the market. Ibn Khatal was caught while clinging to the curtains of the Kaaba; Sa'id ibn Hurayth and Ammar ibn Yasir raced toward him, and Sa'id preceded Ammar and killed him.

The narrator said: Abu Sufyan rushed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and took hold of his stirrup (غرزه), kissed it, and then said: 'May my father and mother be sacrificed for you! Do you not hear what Sa'd is saying? He is saying:

Today is the day of slaughter Today the sanctity shall be violated

The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to Ali (peace be upon him): 'Overtake him and take the banner from him, and be the one who enters with it, and enter it with a gentle entry.' So Ali (peace be upon him) took it and entered as he was commanded.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered Mecca, the chieftains of the Quraysh entered the Kaaba, thinking that the sword would not be lifted from them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came and stood at the door of the Kaaba and said: 'There is no god but Allah, alone, alone. He fulfilled His promise, helped His servant, and defeated the confederates alone. Behold, every property, claim of glory (مأثرة), or blood that is claimed is under these two feet of mine, except the custodianship of the Kaaba and the providing of water to the pilgrims; for these are returned to their people. Behold, Mecca is sacred by the sanctification of Allah; it was not lawful for anyone before me, and it was not made lawful for me except for one hour of a day. It is sacred until the Hour is established: its fresh herbage shall not be cut, its trees shall not be felled, its game shall not be flushed, and its lost property is not lawful except for one who announces it.'

Then he said: 'Behold, what evil neighbors of the Prophet you were! You denied, expelled, drove out, and harmed. Then you were not satisfied until you came to me in my lands to fight me. Go, for you are the "released ones" (الطلقاء).' The people went out as if they had been raised from the graves and entered into Islam. Allah the Glorified had empowered him over their necks by force, so they were his spoil (فيئاً); for this reason, the people of Mecca were called 'the released ones' (الطلقاء).

Ibn al-Ziba'ra came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), embraced Islam, and said:

O Messenger of the God, indeed my tongue Is mending what I tore when I was perishing (1) When I was following (2) the Satan in the paths (3) Of error, and whoever inclines to his inclination is destroyed My flesh and bones have believed in my Lord Then my soul; you are the witness and the warner

He said: And from Ibn Mas'ud, who said: 'The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered on the day of the Conquest, and around the House were three hundred and sixty idols. He began to strike them with a stick in his hand, saying: جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَما يُبْدِئُ الْباطِلُ وَما يُعِيدُ جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْباطِلُ ـ إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً — "Truth has come, and falsehood can neither originate [anything] nor restore [it]. Truth has come, and falsehood has vanished. Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish."'

And from Ibn Abbas, who said: 'When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) arrived at Mecca, he refused to enter the House while the idols were in it. He ordered them to be removed, and they were taken out, along with the images of Ibrahim and Isma'il (peace be upon them) with divining arrows (الأزلام) in their hands. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "May Allah kill them! By Allah, they knew well that these two never sought omens with them."'

I say: The narrations regarding the story of the Conquest are numerous. Whoever wishes to investigate them thoroughly should consult the books of biography (السير) and the compendiums of reports (جوامع الأخبار); what has been presented is like a summary of them.
